docs.title: Underwater Telephone (UWT) Maintenance Portal
docs.text: Welcome to the digital maintenance manual for the DSIT White Pointer UWT. This site is designed to provide quick access to system specifications, health checks, and repair procedures.

docs.title: 🗂️ Quick Navigation
docs.text: Select a category below to begin:

docs.text: Contains the system purpose, scope, and the Technical Glossary for all acronyms and definitions.

docs.title: 2. Hardware Units

System Status: * Manual Version: 1.0 * Release Date: 06.02.2026 * Security: Commercial in Confidence

docs.title: 1. Introduction
docs.text: This chapter serves as an introduction to maintaining the DSIT underwater telephone (UWT). It details the purpose and scope of the document as well as including a glossary of acronyms and definitions used and a list of references and applicable documents.

docs.title: 1.1 Purpose
docs.text: This document is the maintenance manual (MM) for the DSIT underwater telephone (UWT). It is intended to be used by maintainers of the underwater telephone.

docs.title: 1.2 Scope
docs.text:
An underwater telephone is a communication device used on manned submersibles and many surface ships. It allows voice or audio tone (such as Morse code) communication through water. The system employs analog modulation of acoustic waves at carrier frequencies suitable for the human ear, making it suitable for acoustic transmission underwater. It is a specialized phone system for communicating beneath the waves!

The DSIT underwater telephone includes user definable parameters (such as frequency) with both active and passive sonar components. It can perform the following functions, including:

  • Telephone
  • Telegraph
  • Message and small file transfer
  • Pinger
  • Distance measurement
  • Noise display
  • System health (telemetry)

The results are presented to the user by display as well as by audio. This guide explains the maintenance of the DSIT White Pointer underwater telephone with all its various options. Therefore, the manual includes the different tasks carried out by the maintainers of the White Pointer underwater telephone with all necessary directions and explanations.

The overall hardware system is presented in this manual both from the point of view of the maintainer and to help understand the UWTs operation.

docs.title: Glossary
docs.text: This section contains the acronyms, abbreviations, and definitions relevant to the DSIT Underwater Telephone (UWT) Maintenance Manual.

docs.title: 1.3.1 Acronyms and Abbreviations
docs.text: Term Meaning A Ampere AC Alternating Current CB Circuit breaker CIC Combat Information Center CONN Connector / Connection dB Decibel DC Direct Current DMM Digital Multi Meter DPGA Dynamically Programmable Gate Array DPM Depth Measurement FPGA Field Programmable Gate Array GUI Graphical User Interface (also referred to as HMI and MMI) HF High frequency HMI Human Machine Interface HMS Hull Mounted Sonar Hz Hertz LAN Local Area Network LF Low frequency LRU Line Replaceable Unit MIC Microphone MM Maintenance manual MMI Man Machine Interface NATO North Atlantic Treaty Organization OS Operating System PA Power Amplifier PPC Panel Personal Computer PTT Push To Talk RX Receive or Reception SSB Single SideBand TX Transmit or Transmission

docs.title: Technical Terms
docs.text:
Carrier frequency The frequency of a carrier wave, measured in cycles per second, or Hertz, that is modulated to transmit signals.

Demodulation The process by which modulated electrical/digital signals are converted back into the original unmodulated data/information.

Hydrophone An underwater microphone that detects sound waves.

LRU (Line Replaceable Unit) A modular component designed to be replaced without specialized tools.

Ping An acoustic pulse.

Transducer Active sonar transducer that emits an acoustic signal or pulse of sound into the water.

docs.title: Hardware Controls
docs.text:
Rotary Control Knob * Adjusts any parameter value that uses + /– icons. * The knob is normally latched; rotating it unlatches it. * Pressing the knob closes the current menu level and returns to the previous menu while saving the selected value. * If not pressed, the knob re-latches automatically after 3 seconds.

Single Sideband (SSB) * A form of amplitude modulation (AM) that transmits only one sideband (upper or lower). * Cuts bandwidth in half and saves power compared to conventional AM. * Provides better signal efficiency and reduced noise.

docs.title: 2. Transducer Unit
docs.text:
Both the transducer unit projectors and the hydrophone are mounted on a plate, as shown in Figure 1.

![Transducer-Unit](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stefanie-saffern/uwt-maintenance-manual/main/images/hardware/transducer-unit.png")

The plate is mounted under the array as shown below.

![Transducers under HMS Array](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stefanie-saffern/uwt-maintenance-manual/main/images/hardware/UWT-transducers-under-hms-array.png)

Three cables exit the transducers and are terminated by an underwater connector. These are connected to the distribution box via underwater-type extension cables. These cables pass through watertight glands in the HMS sea chest cover, alongside the HMS array cables, as shown in Figure 3.

![Dome and Sea Chest](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stefanie-saffern/uwt-maintenance-manual/main/images/hardware/dome-and-sea-chest.png)

docs.title: 2.1 Low Frequency Projector
docs.text:
The low frequency (LF) projector is a spherical transducer. An isometric view is shown in Figure 4.

![Isometric view Low Frequency Projector](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stefanie-saffern/uwt-maintenance-manual/main/images/hardware/isometric-view-lf-projector.png)

The LF projector measures 165 mm in diameter by 262.5 mm in height, with a weight of 8 kg in air. Technical views and dimensions are provided in Figure 5.

![Technical View Low Frequency Projector](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stefanie-saffern/uwt-maintenance-manual/main/images/hardware/technical-view-lf-projector.jpg)

LF Projector Parameters: - Frequency band: 1 – 18 kHz - Resonance frequency: 9 kHz - Source level (SL) at resonance: 193 dB re 1 µPa@1 m - Input signal level (from distribution box): 110 Vrms - Output acoustic power at resonance: 150 watts

The 14 m extension cable (common to both LF and HF models) is shown in Figure 6.

![Low Frequency High Frequency Extension Cable](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stefanie-saffern/uwt-maintenance-manual/main/images/hardware/lf-hf-projector-extension-cable.png)

docs.title: 2.2 High Frequency Projector
docs.text:
The high frequency (HF) projector is a hemispherical transducer. An isometric view is shown in Figure 7.

![Isometric View of High Frequency Projector](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stefanie-saffern/uwt-maintenance-manual/main/images/hardware/isometric-view-hf-projector.png)

docs.title: 3. Distribution Box
docs.text:
The distribution box (junction box) connects the transducer unit to the transceiver. An isometric view of the distribution box is shown in the following figure, including its two stepdown transformers used for TX (transmit) signals.

![Isometric View of Distribution Box](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stefanie-saffern/uwt-maintenance-manual/main/images/hardware/isometric-view-dist-box.png)

The distribution box dimensions are 280 mm width by 210 mm depth by 390 mm height, with a weight of 11 kg.

The three cables from the transducer unit are connected to the distribution box via three cable glands at the bottom, as shown in the following figure.

![Transducer Unit Cable Connections](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stefanie-saffern/uwt-maintenance-manual/main/images/hardware/transducer-unit-cable-connection.png)

Two cables connect the distribution box to the transceiver. The connectors are on the upper side of the distribution box, as shown in the following figure. The front door opening key is also shown above and in the following figure.

![Transceiver cables connection](https://raw.githubusercontent.com/stefanie-saffern/uwt-maintenance-manual/main/images/hardware/transceiver-cables-connection.png)

The distribution box (junction box) is mounted on a wall using two shock mounts.

docs.title: 3.1 Transformers
docs.text: As stated above, there are two stepdown transformers in the distribution box, one for the LF transmit signal and one for the HF transmit signal. The LF transmit signal transformer reduces the TX signal level from 220 Vrms (the power amplifier output) to 110 Vrms...

docs.title: 4. Transceiver
docs.text: The transceiver implements all UWT transmission and receiving functions, including the MMI (man-machine interface). The transceiver dimensions are 540 mm width by 460 mm depth by 340 mm height, with a weight of 48 kg. From the user’s viewpoint, the transceiver is the heart of DSIT’s White Pointer UWT and is shown in the following figure.

docs.title: 4.1 Transceiver User Devices
docs.text:
As previously explained, the transceiver includes several user devices, including a headset with a microphone, a microphone with PTT (push to talk switch), and a Morse key. These were presented in chapter Error! Reference source not found., Error! Reference source not found.

The following figure shows the transceiver with these operation and activation devices mounted on the front panel. The picture is a copy of Figure 2: White Pointer but with the addition of labels for all the different user devices, activation devices, and their connectors. From the figure, the transceiver is mounted on a metal plate and has four shock mounts (elastic suspensions) on the lower side. The front panel is a PC (personal computer) and includes a touch screen, called a panel PC (PPC). Behind the PPC, there is a chassis with electronic cards mounted on a backplane.

The above labels are explained in the following table:

# Type Tag Location and function 1 Button PWR The power button on the top left is red when off. When turned on, it flashes orange, then turns green. 2 Connector Headset The headset connector on the bottom right connects to the headphones. 3 Device PTT The PTT (push to talk) switch is located between the headphone and headset connector. 4 Connector Morse The Morse connector, on the left of the headset, connects to the Morse keyer. 5 Connector PTT MIC Tangent The PTT MIC tangent connector to the left of the Morse connector allows two full voice and listening devices to be connected simultaneously. 6 Connector USB The USB connector allows USB devices to be connected, such as a mouse. 7 Knob On the front right side of the White Pointer is the rotary control knob used to adjust parameters. 8 Knob Speaker The speaker volume control knob adjusts the speaker sound level. 9 Knob Headphones The headphones volume adjusts the headphones sound level.

Table 1: White Pointer (transceiver) front controls and connectors

docs.title: System Health Checks
docs.text: No routine preventive maintenance is required for the UWT system. However, it is highly recommended to perform regular system health checks to ensure operational readiness.

docs.title: 6.1 System Health Check in the Harbor
docs.text: Frequency: Three hours before sailing or every two months (whichever is earlier). Personnel: Level 1 maintainer (e.g., Sonar Operator or Onboard Technician).

docs.title: Procedure Checklist:
docs.text:
1. [ ] System Power: Switch on the system and confirm initialization.
  2. [ ] Noise Mode: Open Noise Mode and verify that a typical noise spectrum is displayed on the PPC.
  3. [ ] Audio Verification: Listen to the headphones and speaker; verify that typical acoustic noise audio is audible.
  4. [ ] DPGA Telemetry Check:
    • Open the telemetry DPGA display format.
    • Verify that all health status indicators are GREEN (OK).
  5. [ ] PA Telemetry (8 kHz):
    • Open the telemetry PA display format.
    • Send an audio message at 8 kHz, speaking into the microphone for 10 seconds.
    • Verify all health status data remains GREEN.
  6. [ ] PA Telemetry (37 kHz):
    • Open the telemetry PA display format.
    • Send an audio message at 37 kHz, speaking into the microphone for 10 seconds.
    • Verify all health status data remains GREEN.

docs.title: 6.2 System Health Checks at Sea
docs.text:
Health checks at sea are required to ensure the transducers are performing correctly under operational conditions.

Frequency: Every 24 hours while at sea. Operational Condition: Must be performed in water depths greater than 50 meters. Personnel: Level 1 maintainer.

docs.title: Procedure:
docs.text:
The task steps are identical to the Harbor Health Check (Steps 1–6 above).

Note: A Level 1 Maintainer (Sonar Operator or Onboard Technician) must perform this task continually every 24 hours while the vessel is at sea.

docs.title: Maintenance Summary Table
docs.text: Check Type Frequency Water Depth Requirement Harbor Check 3hrs before sail / 2 Months Any All Telemetry GREEN At-Sea Check Every 24 Hours > 50 Meters All Telemetry GREEN

docs.title: Troubleshooting and Repairs
docs.text: Failures in the UWT system are detected via the Panel PC (PPC) or by monitoring the DPGA/PA card telemetry displays.

docs.title: 7.1 Failure Detected by the User
docs.text: The user can typically detect three main faults during operation:

docs.title: 1. Transceiver does not switch on
docs.text: Action: 1. Check if 230 VAC is connected to the transceiver unit J1 connector in the rear panel. 2. Verify the circuit breaker in the rear panel is not tripped (pushed down). If it is, push it up. 3. If the circuit breaker trips again, the AC-DC power supply is likely faulty and must be replaced (Section 8.3.2.1). 4. If 230 VAC is present but the unit remains off, check for 24 VDC output from the power supply using a DVM.

docs.title: 2. The display is blank
docs.text: Action: * If the system is powered on but the screen is dark, the PPC is faulty. * Replacement: Follow instructions in Section 8.3.1 for PPC replacement.

docs.title: 3. No audio is heard
docs.text: If no audio is heard in the headset/speaker and there is no noise display, check the following LRUs in order of priority: 1. DPGA Card (First Priority) 2. PPC (Second Priority) 3. Hydrophone (Third Priority)

docs.title: DPGA Telemetry (Section 7.2)
docs.text: If any item in the DPGA telemetry display is RED, the DPGA card has failed and must be replaced.

docs.title: PA Telemetry (Section 7.3)
docs.text:
Refer to the table below for Power Amplifier status:

Condition Meaning Action HVPS entry is red HVPS card failure Replace HVPS card Any PA telemetry is red PA card failure Replace PA card LF entries red (during TX) LF projector failure Replace LF Projector HF entries red (during TX) HF projector failure Replace HF Projector

WARNING: Before disassembling any Line Replaceable Unit (LRU), switch the system OFF and disconnect the input electric power cable (AC or DC).

docs.title: 1 transceiver unit
docs.text:
8.3 Transceiver Unit The transceiver unit includes the following LRUs: • PPC • AC-DC power supply • HVPS card • PA card • DPGA card • Backplane • AC and DC power components (EMI filters, circuit breaker, contactors) • Fans 8.3.1 PPC replacement To replace the PPC: 1. Remove the 16 bolts that hold the PPC in the transceiver housing. 2. Pull the PPC slightly and disconnect the three cables that connect it to the DPGA card as shown in the following figure.

Figure 47: PPC removal 3. Remove the PPC and install a new replacement one in the opposite sequence to its removal. 8.3.2 Removing other LRUs inside the transceiver To remove any other LRU inside the transceiver, remove the PPC first to access the inside LRUs as shown in the following figure.

Figure 48: Internal LRUs of the transceiver To replace any card (HVPS, PA, DPGA): 1. Remove the PPC as described in section ‎8.3.1, PPC replacement, above. 2. Disconnect the cables connected to the LRU by opening the front panel connectors. 3. Open the bolts that hold the LRU. 4. Remove the LRU and install the new replacement one in the opposite sequence to its removal. 8.3.2.1 Replacing the AC_DC power supply To replace the AC-DC power supply and the AC and DC power components: 1. Remove the PPC as described in section ‎8.3.1, PPC replacement, above. 2. Disconnect the cables connected to the LRU. (The cable wires are connected to the LRU by bolts.) 3. Open the bolts that hold the LRU. 4. Remove the LRU and install the new replacement one in the opposite sequence to its removal. 8.3.2.2 Replacing a fan To replace a fan: 1. Remove the PPC as described in section ‎8.3.1, PPC replacement, above. 2. Remove the upper panel covering the fans by opening the four bolts of the upper panel. 3. Disconnect the fan cable. 4. Open the four bolts holding the fan to the transceiver housing ceiling. 5. Remove the fan and install the new replacement one in the opposite sequence to its removal. 8.3.2.3 Replacing the backplane To replace the backplane: 1. Disconnect all cables connected to the transceiver unit rear connectors. 2. Remove the PPC as described in section ‎8.3.1, PPC replacement, above. 3. Remove the three LRU cards: HVPS, PA and DPGA as described in section ‎8.3.2, Removing other LRUs inside the transceiver. 4. Remove the two fans as described in section ‎8.3.2.2, Replacing a fan. 5. Remove any mechanical bracket that blocks the backplane. 6. Disconnect the cables between the backplane and all remaining parts (the AC-DC power supply and the AC and DC power components). 7. Open the four bolts holding the backplane to the transceiver housing back wall. 8. Remove the backplane and install the new replacement one in the opposite sequence to its removal.

docs.title: 2 distribution box
docs.text:
8.2 Distribution Box To replace the two transformers: 1. Disconnect the two wires of the projector from the transformer. 2. Open all four bolts that hold the transformer in the distribution box as shown in the following figure (at the corners of the transformer).

Figure 46: Transformer 3. Remove the transformer and install a new replacement transformer in the opposite sequence to its removal.

docs.title: Troubleshooting and Replacement Procedures
docs.text:
When there is a failure in the system, the troubleshooting procedures of the previous chapter will detect the failed part, namely the line replaceable unit (LRU). The failed LRU should be replaced by a new one from the spare parts set. This chapter provides information and instructions on how to disassemble a failed LRU and reassemble a new LRU.

⚠ WARNING: Important safety instructions! Before disassembling an LRU in the system, switch it off and disconnect the input electric power cable (AC or DC sources).

docs.title: 8.1 Transducer Unit
docs.text: The transducer unit is mounted below the HMS array, as shown in . To replace any transducer (projector, hydrophone), the HMS array must be lifted into the ship’s hull to provide access to the transducer unit. This must be done according to the HMS maintenance manual.

docs.title: To access and replace a transducer:
docs.text:
1. Locate the transducers installed on a plate secured to the HMS array frame with four bolts.
  2. Open the four bolts securing the plate to the array frame to remove the mounting plate.
  3. Pull the plate downward carefully to access the upper side of the mounting plate and the transducer assembly.

Open the four bolts to remove the mounting plate from the array frame and pull the plate carefully downwards to get access to the plate’s upper side.

The following figure shows the transducer unit plate from the underneath.

docs.title: 8.1.1 LF Projector
docs.text:
To disassemble the LF projector: 1. Open the six bolts that hold the LF projector mounting flange to remove the LF projector from the mounting plate, as shown in the following figure.

2. Pull the LF projector downwards carefully and disconnect its connector. 3. Disassemble the mounting flange from the LF projector by opening six bolts, as shown in the following figure.

4. Remove the LF projector and install the new replacement LF projector in the opposite sequence to its removal.

docs.title: 8.1.2 HF Projector
docs.text:
To disassemble the HF projector: 1. Open the six bolts that hold the HF projector mounting flange to remove the HF projector from the mounting plate, as shown in the following figure.

2. Pull the HF projector downwards carefully and disconnect its connector. 3. Disassemble the mounting flange from the HF projector by opening six bolts as shown in the above figure. 4. Remove the HF projector and install the new replacement HF projector in the opposite sequence to its removal.

docs.title: 8.1.3 Hydrophone
docs.text:
To disassemble the hydrophone: 1. Remove the two bolts that hold the hydrophone to its mounting device, as shown in the following figure.

2. Disconnect the hydrophone connector and remove the hydrophone. 3. Install the new replacement hydrophone in the opposite sequence to its removal.
